posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: I had a 17 gouges porrets last night which was beautiful, earthy, and lush with super pure fruits and a nice finish. It was a lovely wine but not at all what I expected. What other producers are more accessible now, Roty? Faiveley?

posts.name: Phil T r o t t i e r
posts.cooked:
I had a 17 gouges porrets last night which was beautiful, earthy, and lush with super pure fruits and a nice finish. It was a lovely wine but not at all what I expected. What other producers are more accessible now, Roty? Faiveley?

Sorry, not answering the question but even though it drinks well in its infancy would you say it has the backbone to age well in the “long term” (15 years)? I have some that I purchased but did not try and they are buried for aging.

Yes, definitely. Will it go 50 years? Who knows, but I don’t care. 99 porrets wasn’t close to ready last year.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Don’t drink much Gouges so have not had the wine, but how much of this reflects the 2017 vintage rather than the producer?

Virtually every good Burgundy I have had is good in 15 years. Where this has not been the case (say a few 90s), it has not been because the wine is too old but because the wine gets a strange flavor or something.

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked: I am in line with Howard here …which means the vintage 2017 is speaking ( rather the producer ).

posts.name: Мајкл Ристев
posts.cooked: in general there seems to be a large shift towards more aromatic, expressive, accessible styles. perhaps it is a reaction to the chunky, extracted and oaky wines of the late 90s to early 00s that have seemingly not aged so gracefully. however, part of me doubts if these newer wines that tend more toward the direction of infusion will fare any better with time in the cellar than their aesthetic predecessors (obviously for the exact opposite of reasons).

posts.name: J0hn
posts.cooked: I don’t have any experience tasting Gouges yet, but have read several tasting notes and forum posts alluding to a change in style at Gouges in recent years. So it’s probably not solely attributable to the vintage.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Answering the question in a different way, I’d have to say the shift towards longer macerations that really took off in the mid-19th century. Eighteenth-century Burgundy was generally made with 24-36-hour macerations and only “kept” for a few years. For a variety of reasons, this changed in the following century, with month-long macerations being quite typical. It’s hard to exaggerate what a fundamental aesthetic difference this must have made to wine styles. It was also debated at the time: in 1860, Guyot was deploring the trend for longer and longer macerations, arguing that two weeks was more than ample. I don’t think any of the swings of the stylistic pendulum in the 20th and 21st centuries can match the change that took place between 1790 and 1890.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
I am in line with Howard here …which means the vintage 2017 is speaking ( rather the producer ).

Gouges has significantly changed style, it’s not just 2017.

I’ve heard the same. Do you know happen to know when the changes started? I have bottles starting with 2014, but haven’t opened any yet.

Started around 2008 but has been gradual going forward. Gregory Gouges has said he wanted to make the wines more approachable in their youth (among some other changes).

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
Greg…thanks. Nice to learn around 2008.

I need of find a reason to open his Les Pruliers 2010

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Historical footnote, but two 1998 Gouges in the last three weeks have shown utterly differently:

Chenes Carteaux: On the near end of its drinking window, with generous fruit; opening up nicely. A pleasure to drink. Notes posted here.

After that experience, I decided to try the Les Chaignots last night. A different story: Very old-style Gouge-ish. Extracted, moderately tannic, a tad coarse, with little fruit. No joy here.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: For recent years, I would likely cite climate change over any specific producer shift, as its effects go across the region. Just look at how people react to riper style vintages (e.g. 2018 versus the more classically styled 2017) to see how it influences everything.

posts.name: Scott Brunson
posts.cooked:
Answering the question in a different way, I’d have to say the shift towards longer macerations that really took off in the mid-19th century. Eighteenth-century Burgundy was generally made with 24-36-hour macerations and only “kept” for a few years. For a variety of reasons, this changed in the following century, with month-long macerations being quite typical. It’s hard to exaggerate what a fundamental aesthetic difference this must have made to wine styles. It was also debated at the time: in 1860, Guyot was deploring the trend for longer and longer macerations, arguing that two weeks was more than ample. I don’t think any of the swings of the stylistic pendulum in the 20th and 21st centuries can match the change that took place between 1790 and 1890.

That and adding a “touch” of hermitage from Algeria?

fify
I agree that Gouges (and Chandon de Briailles) wines have become more approachable young, but I have not found the same trait in Faiveley.

Do you mean there’s been no change over the last 15 years? Erwan’s wines are extremely different from Francois’; the oak treatment alone would radically set them apart.

posts.name: Ryan Caughey
posts.cooked:
Faiveley and Gouges are the obvious names to me. Faiveley in particular is night-and-day. But there are others. I’ve heard many times that some previously oaky producers like Dugat-Py and Perrot Minot have dialed it back. I don’t have enough personal experience to say much on the issue.

Out of curiosity, last night I opened an '18 Gouges Les Saint Georges and it was surprisingly textured and approachable (and super delicious, in a darker/bluer-fruited sort of way) while still having plenty of structure to age.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:

Out of curiosity, last night I opened an '18 Gouges Les Saint Georges and it was surprisingly textured and approachable (and super delicious, in a darker/bluer-fruited sort of way) while still having plenty of structure to age.

Dugat-Py is another great example. Wish the wines were cheaper.

posts.name: Mike Evans
posts.cooked: Earlier this year I had 2008 and 2017 Gouges LSG within about a week of each other and both were surprisingly open, friendly, and approachable.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Answering the question in a different way, I’d have to say the shift towards longer macerations that really took off in the mid-19th century. Eighteenth-century Burgundy was generally made with 24-36-hour macerations and only “kept” for a few years. For a variety of reasons, this changed in the following century, with month-long macerations being quite typical. It’s hard to exaggerate what a fundamental aesthetic difference this must have made to wine styles. It was also debated at the time: in 1860, Guyot was deploring the trend for longer and longer macerations, arguing that two weeks was more than ample. I don’t think any of the swings of the stylistic pendulum in the 20th and 21st centuries can match the change that took place between 1790 and 1890.

That and adding a “touch” of hermitage?

Sometimes the term “hermitagé” was used to refer to wines cut with other, more robust wines; but the actual use of Hermitage / northern Rhône as a blending wine was mainly in Bordeaux. In Burgundy, Châteauneuf was the “medicine” of choice for those with aspirations to quality.

fify
I agree that Gouges (and Chandon de Briailles) wines have become more approachable young, but I have not found the same trait in Faiveley.

Algerian wine really came into play as a blending component in the 1870s, in the aftermath of phylloxera, not so much before.

posts.name: Vincent Fritzsche
posts.cooked: A couple years back someone opened a 1er Cru from Chevillon from I think 2014 and I was shocked at the oaky gushy fruit, seemed totally different from the producer I knew. Was checking this thread to see if anyone mentioned Chevillon but I didn’t see it. Any corroboration or dispute? This one bottle tasted intact and (apprently) correct.

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked: Michael, Greg and John have the Gouges stylistic change nailed here, and it applies across the portfolio and it is not dependent upon vintage, as corroborated by Mike’s LSG comments. It is impossible to miss if tasting the wines. Although I expect that the wines will age and improve just fine, they now are much more open, supple, expressive and even more lightly-colored and red-fruited than in decades past.
